concept_name: Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor in Magnetic Field

variables.L: Length of the conductor in the field (m)
variables.theta: Angle between current direction (L) and B
variables.I1: Current in wire 1 (A) — for parallel wires
variables.I2: Current in wire 2 (A) — for parallel wires

locked_facts: Force on a current-carrying conductor: F = IL × B, magnitude F = BIL sin θ
Direction of force given by Fleming's left-hand rule (or cross product IL × B)
Force per unit length between two parallel wires: F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd)
Parallel currents (same direction) ATTRACT each other
Anti-parallel currents (opposite direction) REPEL each other
Definition of ampere: 1 ampere is that constant current which, flowing in two infinite parallel conductors 1 m apart in vacuum, produces a force of 2 × 10⁻⁷ N per metre of length
Force is zero when current direction is parallel to B (θ = 0°)
truth_statements: The force on a straight conductor of length L carrying current I in a uniform magnetic field B is F = IL × B, with the force perpendicular to both the current direction and the field
Two long parallel conductors carrying currents in the same direction attract each other, while conductors carrying currents in opposite directions repel each other
The force per unit length between two infinitely long parallel conductors separated by distance d is F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd)
The SI unit of current (ampere) is defined using the force between parallel current-carrying conductors — making it a fundamental definition linked to the force law
The net force on a current loop in a uniform magnetic field is zero, but there is a net torque that tends to align the loop's magnetic moment with the field

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two parallel wires with current in the same direction. Wire 1 creates a circular magnetic field — at the location of wire 2, this field points toward wire 1. The force IL × B on wire 2 is therefore directed TOWARD wire 1 (attraction). Then reverse wire 2's current — the cross product reverses, giving repulsion. Show the field lines between the wires bunching together (repulsion) or cancelling (attraction).
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Arrow decomposition: wire 1's field at wire 2's location → cross product with wire 2's current → force toward/away from wire 1. The field pattern between the wires (bunched = repulsion, sparse = attraction) provides the visual intuition.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students memorise 'same direction attracts' but cannot explain WHY — seeing the cross product decomposition with the field from the other wire makes the mechanism clear

simulation_states.label: Conductor in external magnetic field
simulation_states.description: A straight conductor carrying current I placed in a uniform magnetic field B. The current direction and field direction are clearly marked with an angle θ between them. No force shown yet.
simulation_states.emphasis: A wire with current I sits in a uniform field B. The angle between IL and B determines the force magnitude.

simulation_states.label: Force appears — F = IL × B
simulation_states.description: The force vector F appears perpendicular to both IL and B. Fleming's left-hand rule is shown as an overlay (forefinger = B, middle finger = I, thumb = F). The magnitude F = BIL sin θ is displayed. When θ = 90°, F is maximum; when θ = 0°, F = 0.
simulation_states.emphasis: The force is always perpendicular to both the current and the field. Use Fleming's left-hand rule or the cross product to find its direction.

simulation_states.label: Parallel wires — attract or repel
simulation_states.description: Two long parallel wires with currents I₁ and I₂. Wire 1's circular field at wire 2's position is shown. The cross product IL₂ × B₁ gives force toward wire 1 (attraction for same-direction currents). Toggle to anti-parallel: force reverses to repulsion. Formula: F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd).
simulation_states.emphasis: Same-direction currents ATTRACT (field lines cancel between wires). Opposite-direction currents REPEL (field lines bunch between wires). This defines the ampere!

simulation_states.label: Interactive exploration
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts I₁, I₂, distance d, and can flip current directions. Force per unit length updates in real time. A toggle switches between single-wire-in-field mode (adjust θ) and parallel-wire mode. Force vectors and field patterns update live.
simulation_states.emphasis: Flip one current direction to switch attract ↔ repel. Halve the distance to double the force. The definition of 1 ampere: I₁ = I₂ = 1 A, d = 1 m → F/L = 2 × 10⁻⁷ N/m.

common_misconceptions.belief: Parallel current-carrying wires repel each other because like things repel
trigger_phrases: parallel repel hota hai jaise like charges
common_misconceptions.correction: Unlike charges, parallel (same-direction) currents ATTRACT each other. This is because wire 1 creates a magnetic field at wire 2, and the cross product F = IL₂ × B₁ points TOWARD wire 1. The analogy with 'like charges repel' does not apply here — magnetic forces follow different rules from electrostatic forces.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the field from wire 1 at wire 2's position, compute the cross product direction explicitly, and demonstrate the attractive force with both wires moving toward each other

common_misconceptions.belief: The force on the conductor is in the direction of the magnetic field
trigger_phrases: conductor B ke direction mein jaata hai
common_misconceptions.correction: The force F = IL × B is PERPENDICULAR to both the current direction and the magnetic field. It is never along B. The direction is given by the cross product or Fleming's left-hand rule.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show I, B, and F as three mutually perpendicular vectors — the force arrow is always perpendicular to the field, never along it
